title: Chapter Twelve: The Public
content: The Great Hall of Blackwood Academy had been born for nights like this.

I stood at the top of the main staircase and let myself look — really look — at what money could do when it had nowhere else to go. Candelabras lined the gallery, real wax, real flame, their light pooling across white linen tablecloths that probably cost more than my mother's mortgage. The string quartet in the corner was playing something baroque and breathless, violin strings catching the candlelight like spun gold. The air smelled of champagne and gardenias and the particular starched formality of people who had paid forty thousand dollars a year to learn how to be cruel with better vocabulary.
I was wearing white.

Not ivory, not cream — white. A column of silk that clung to my shoulders and fell straight to the floor, slit to mid-thigh, back low enough to show the ridge of my spine. My hair was swept up, my mother's pearls at my throat, my mouth painted the exact shade of surrender. When I'd seen myself in the mirror before leaving my room, I'd thought: *virgin sacrifice*. The kind of girl they dressed in white before they led her to the altar, or the pyre — same difference, at Blackwood.
Julian appeared at the bottom of the stairs.

Navy suit. Gold tie. His hair caught the candlelight and held it, and he looked up at me with an expression I'd seen on men's faces before — wonder, want, the desperate hope that the woman walking toward them is exactly who she appears to be. He offered his hand as I descended the last steps, and his fingers closed around mine like a promise he thought I'd keep.
"You look beautiful tonight," he said.
*I look like a weapon wrapped in silk.*
"Thank you," I said, and smiled.
The performance began the moment we crossed the threshold into the hall, and I let myself become what the room needed me to be.

I laughed at Dean Whitmore's joke about the endowment — bright, musical, precisely calibrated to suggest I found him charming without being desperate. I shook hands with the Castellanis, old money, old ruthlessness, and asked after their daughter in Vienna with the warmth of someone who had been friends with her for years rather than someone who had memorized her file three weeks ago. When Reginald Pope — shipping empire, three divorces, wandering hands — leaned too close and asked what a "smart girl like me" saw in young Linton, I tilted my champagne flute toward him like a shield and said, "I see someone who knows how to stay interested," with a smile that was all teeth and no threat, so he laughed and thought I was clever, and his hand never made it past my elbow.
The champagne tasted like carbonated starlight, cold and bright and just bitter enough to remind me it was poison.

Julian's hand rested on the small of my back — warm, steady, proprietary. The silk transmitted every degree of his touch, his palm a brand I could feel even through the fabric. I leaned into it because that was the choreography. I tilted my head toward him when he spoke because that was the framing. I made him look like the luckiest man in the room, and the room took note, and that was the point.
He watched me work with something like awe.

Every time I deflected a question, every time I turned a potential slight into a shared joke, every time I placed the exact right name with the exact right face, I could feel his attention on me — reverent, almost disbelieving, like a man who'd bought a painting and was only now realizing it might be a masterpiece.
He thought he was watching the woman he was falling in love with.
I knew I was rehearsing for the rest of my life.
The mezzanine circled the upper edge of the Great Hall like a private shadow, and that was where Heath was standing.

I found him without looking — the way you find the edge of a cliff, the way the body knows before the mind does that there is danger nearby. He was leaning against the railing with a glass of champagne caught between his fingers, untouched, the bubbles going flat. He wasn't watching the room. He was watching Julian's hand on my back.

I knew the exact moment he registered it because something in his jaw shifted — a fractional tightening, the kind of small tell that would be invisible to anyone who hadn't spent hours memorizing the architecture of his face. The casual possession of it. The proprietary ease. Julian's thumb tracing a small circle against my silk-covered spine, utterly unaware that it was being witnessed, catalogued, burned into memory.
Heath would replay that image for three years. The hand. The circular motion. The way I leaned into it like I belonged there.
I glanced up.
Our eyes met.

One second. Maybe less. A full stop in the middle of a sentence that had been running since September. His face was perfectly, brutally blank — not anger, not longing, not any of the things that would have been forgivable. Nothing. A wall so high and so smooth you could climb it forever and never find a handhold.

My face was everything I couldn't show. Every unsent letter, every hallway I'd walked past his door, every night I'd pressed my palm against the cold glass of my window and imagined it was his hand pressing back. All of it lived in that one second, hidden behind my smile, trapped in the space between what I was and what I was pretending to be.
I looked away first.
I always looked away first.
"Dance with me," Julian said.

It wasn't a question — not quite — but his voice held enough uncertainty that it could have been. I smiled and let him lead me to the floor because it was expected, because the quartet was playing something slow and aching, because three hundred people were watching and this was part of the performance.

His hand settled on my waist. My hand went to his shoulder. Our other hands joined, and we moved, and it was easy — too easy, the way everything with Julian was easy, the way swimming is easy when you're drifting with the current and haven't noticed the waterfall yet.
He pulled me closer. I let him. His breath was warm against my temple, and he smelled of cedar and something clean, like fresh linens on a cold morning — a good-boy scent, uncomplicated and safe.
"I keep waiting for you to feel uncomfortable with this," he murmured. "You never do."
The words landed softly, casually, the way he meant them — an observation, maybe a compliment. He didn't know they were a door I'd been holding shut with both hands.
"I was raised for this," I said.

It came out too honest. Too quick. I felt the shape of it in my mouth like a swallowed key, and for one terrible second I thought he might ask the follow-up question, the one that would crack me open like an egg.
"Is that a good thing?"

He asked it gently. Curiously. The kind of question a man asks when he wants to understand you better, when he wants to close the distance between what he sees and what is true. He could have pushed. He could have said *what do you mean, raised for this?* He could have looked at me — really looked — and seen the seams showing.

But Julian was a good boy, and good boys don't push. Good boys sense the edge of something uncomfortable and step back, because pressing would be rude, and they would rather live with confusion than be the cause of a moment's discomfort.
So he asked *is that a good thing?* and then he waited, patient and golden, for an answer I couldn't give.
I smiled instead.

The smile was the most dangerous thing in the room. It was warm and present and full of manufactured light. It told him everything was fine, I was fine, we were fine, and it closed the door he'd cracked open with the finality of a vault. He smiled back, relieved, and we kept dancing, and I held him the way you hold something you're afraid to crush and afraid to drop.
The bathroom was on the second floor, down a hallway the catering staff hadn't found.

I made my excuses — touching Julian's arm, murmuring something about fresh air, laughing at my own interruption as if I found the whole production delightfully exhausting rather than existentially necessary. He nodded, solicitous, and I walked away from him and the light and the music and the three hundred people who thought they knew who I was, and I found the bathroom, and I locked the door, and I pressed my back against it like something was chasing me.
My hands were shaking.

I held them up and watched them tremble — fine, barely visible, the kind of shake that would be invisible to anyone who wasn't looking for it. But I was looking. I was always looking. I pressed my right palm flat against my left and felt the scar there, the thin raised line where the skin had knitted itself back together after we'd sworn our pact in blood and fire and the desperate conviction of children who thought promises could protect them.
*I'm still me. I'm still me.*

I whispered it like a prayer. Like an incantation. Like the words themselves had power, could reach back through the evening and the performance and the three hundred lies I'd told with my eyes and my hands and my painted mouth, and find the girl underneath. The one who existed before the choreography. The one who remembered what it felt like to say something true.
*I'm still me.*

My eyes burned. I blinked the heat away. I turned to the mirror above the sink and saw myself — the white dress, the swept hair, the pearls, the mouth. A girl built for rooms like the one I'd just left. A weapon wrapped in silk, polished and dangerous and absolutely nothing like the person I'd wanted to be.
I straightened the dress. I fixed the smile. I unlocked the door and walked back to the gala, and no one — not one person in that room — saw the difference.
The last notes of the quartet died sometime after midnight.

The candles had burned low, wax pooling in silver holders. The champagne bottles stood empty like a forest of green glass. The donors were gathering their coats, pressing final hands, making promises they might or might not keep. I stood beside Julian at the door and thanked people for coming and laughed at the right moments and did not look up at the mezzanine, because Heath was already gone.
Julian walked me back to my residence hall.

The night was cold, the kind of spring cold that pretends to be warm and then bites you when you stop paying attention. The path crunched under our shoes, and the moon was half-full and indifferent, and Julian held my hand the way he'd held it all night — gently, confidently, like a man who had every reason to be confident.
We stopped at my door.

He kissed my forehead. Gentle. Appropriate. A kiss that said *I respect you* and *I care about you* and *I am not the kind of man who would push for more on a night like this.* A kiss designed to make me feel safe.
It did not make me feel safe. It made me feel like a house with all the lights on and no one home.
"Goodnight, Cat," he said.
"Goodnight, Julian."
He walked away, and I went inside, and I closed the door, and I stood in the dark of my empty room and did not cry.

I never cried. Not anymore. Crying was a luxury for people who had the time to feel things, and I had a performance to maintain, and the performance never ended, and the shakes in my hands would go away if I just kept them busy enough.
I went to the window instead.

The conservatory was out there somewhere, beyond the dark lawn, beyond the reach of the security lights. I could see its shadow — the glass roof, the arched frames, the place where the moonlight hit the old piano and made the keys glow like teeth. Dark tonight. Empty. The way it always was when I needed it not to be.

Heath wasn't there. I knew because I'd checked. I'd looked for the glow of his cigarette, the amber point I'd memorized on other nights, the only lighthouse in the Blackwood dark. Nothing. He was somewhere else tonight, somewhere I couldn't see, carrying the image of Julian's hand on my back like a stone in his chest.
Three people in the same room tonight, and none of us saw the same reality.
Julian saw a woman he loved, brilliant and effortless, everything he'd ever wanted. He saw a future that was bright and certain and clean.
Heath saw the thing he'd lost — or never had, which was worse. He saw a hand on a back, a claim staked, a door that had been closed against him so many times it had finally locked.

And I saw the truth: that I was none of the things they believed I was. I was a performance artist dying inside my own act. I was a girl pressing her scarred palm against a bathroom door and begging herself to stay real. I was standing at a window in a white dress, looking at an empty conservatory, in love with a shadow on a mezzanine while the golden boy slept soundly one building over, dreaming of the woman he thought I was.

The distance between us — between what Julian believed and what Heath knew and what I carried alone — was the entire tragedy, compressed into a single moment. A spring night. A closed door. An empty room where the music used to be.
I turned from the window.
I unpinned my hair. I took off the pearls. I stepped out of the white dress and let it pool on the floor like something shed, like a skin, like the ghost of the girl who'd worn it.
Underneath, I was still me.
*I was still me.*
